retrieved_docs.title: Mandibulofacial Dysostosis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The syndrome was described by Berry (1889), Treacher Collins (1900), and Franceschetti and Klein (1949) ergo the names Berry’s syndrome and Franceschetti-Zwahlen-Klein syndrome are also used in older literature. [8] Treacher Collins syndrome (TCS) is the more preferred modern eponym, as he definitively described the essential components of the condition. [3] Franceschetti and Klein used the term mandibulofacial dysostosis to describe the clinical features, and as the modern literature eschews eponyms whenever possible, this has become a frequent descriptor. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Agenesis of teeth often occurs in association with a syndrome and ranges from the absence of a single tooth to the failure of all the teeth to develop. Milder cases of dental agenesis can typically be restored or addressed by relatively noninvasive measures. Anomalies in the number of teeth are classified as anodontia, hypodontia, oligodontia, or hyperdontia based on the presence or absence of the number of teeth involved.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During normal tooth development, various complications can occur that affect the number of developing teeth. These dental anomalies range from the absence of all teeth (anodontia) to having more teeth than the normal number of teeth (hyperdontia). When describing missing or absent teeth, some may also use the term agenesis, which is the failure of all or part of an organ to develop during embryonic growth. While mild cases of dental agenesis may not even be noticed until later in life, some forms of dental agenesis can be devastating and debilitating, resulting in a poor quality of life in affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortopulmonary Septal Defect -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: An aortopulmonary (AP) septal defect, also known as an AP window, is one of the rarest congenital heart defects accounting for less than 0.5% of forms of congenital heart disease. [1] This defect can occur in isolation or concurrently with other forms of congenital heart disease, such as a ventricular septal defect, interruption of the aortic arch, tetralogy of Fallot, and, rarely, coronary artery anomalies. [2] [3] [4] [5] [6] By definition, the AP window is a direct "side to side" connection of the ascending aorta to the main pulmonary artery but with the formation of a normal aortic valve and a right ventricular outflow tract, differentiating it from truncus arteriosus (see Video. Echocardiogram of Aortopulmonary Window). [7] Embryologically an AP window develops when there is incomplete septation of the great arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: Newborn Screening -- Definition/Introduction -- Pulse Oximetry
retrieved_docs.content: Below is a list of the critical congenital heart defects that are the target of pulse oximetry screening. [4] Pulmonary atresia Tricuspid atresia Truncus arteriosus Total anomalous pulmonary venous return Hypoplastic left heart syndrome D-Transposition of the great vessels Double outlet right ventricle Ebstein anomaly Interrupted aortic arch Single ventricle complex Coarctation of the aorta Tetralogy of Fallot Below is a list of the diseases and disorders included on the Recommended Uniform Screening Panel (RUSP) as of July 2018. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: PHACE Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional group of specialists has established the 2009 diagnostic criteria, which had an update in 2016. The diagnostic basis of definite PS is the presence of a facial hemangioma of more than 5 cm with one major criterion or two minor criteria. Possible PS requires the presence of cervicofacial hemangioma with one minor criterion. [11] Major criteria: Arterial: anomalies of major cerebral or cervical arteries, stenosis, occlusion, dysplasia, hypoplasia, persistent carotid-vertebrobasilar anastomosis Structural brain: posterior fossa anomalies: Danky-Walker complex, unilateral or bilateral cerebral dysplasia or hypoplasia Cardiovascular: aortic arch anomalies, aneurysm, an aberrant origin of the subclavian artery Ocular: posterior segment anomaly, retinal vascular anomalies Ventral or midline: sternal defect, sternal cleft Minor criteria:

retrieved_docs.title: Septate Uterus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 2021, The American Society of Reproductive Medicine (ASRM) updated its Müllerian Anomaly Classification System (MAC2021) to update and expand prior classification systems in a manner that applies to all levels of healthcare providers and promotes patient awareness and advocacy. MAC2021 classifies Müllerian anomalies into 9 categories: Müllerian agenesis, cervical agenesis, unicornuate uterus, uterus didelphys, bicornuate uterus, septate uterus, longitudinal vaginal septum, transverse vaginal septum, and complex anomalies. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cilia Incarnata -- Differential Diagnosis -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: The finding is exactly that seen in cilium incarnatum internum, in that a lash will grow inwards. Historically, this term cilium inversum was used when the condition was congenital. However, it has also been used interchangeably with findings which are exactly those seen in cilium incarnatum internum in adults: Tibbles (in 1923 in the BMJ) first reported of its occurrence in a woman of 21 years, who after having the trouble of irritation for three weeks presented two such in-­growing eyelashes. One was at the posterior border of the lid margin, and the other one appearing in the sub­tarsal sulcus. Duke-Elder (1952) men­tions Schrieber (1924) as the next one to report it. No mention of such cases is found in the literature after that. Authors of this article propose that this term is dropped as it simplifies matters to call the condition "congenital cilium incarnatum internum."

retrieved_docs.title: Williams Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiologist Dr. John Cyprian Phipps Williams first described the syndrome in 1961. In 1962, German physician Dr. A. J. Beuren found similar findings in a series of 3 patients, naming the syndrome Williams-Beuren syndrome, which is still used in some publications. [4] [3] In 1975, the definitive treatise on the syndrome's cardiovascular, behavioral, and systemic effects was published. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Cilia Incarnata -- Differential Diagnosis -- Definition
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertrichosis is an increase in hair beyond the normal variation for a patient's age, sex, or ethnicity. Eyelash hypertrichosis is known as trichomegaly.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hypodontia and oligodontia often fall under the broader classification of “hypodontia,” with specific distinctions made based on the number of missing teeth. Hypodontia represents the most common dentofacial malformation in humans. [7] A clear correlation exists between hypodontia in the deciduous (primary) dentition and the corresponding permanent dentition; patients missing a primary tooth frequently lack its permanent successor. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Holoprosencephaly -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: An MRI is used to diagnose HPE definitively. Depending on the clinical and MRI findings, HPE can be classified into alobar, semilobar, lobar, and middle intrahemispheric variants.

retrieved_docs.title: Dwarfism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dwarfism is the medical terminology for short-stature. It is defined as height-vertex below two standard deviations (-2SD) or in the third percentile for a given age and sex. The standing height is measured from ground to the vertex when the head is in the Frankfurt-horizontal plane. Most people have an aversion to the term dwarf and identify themselves as "Little people" and also have a community under the name -'Little People of America'.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Anomalies -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The accepted definition of an anomaly is a developmental finding that occurs in less than 1% of the population, as opposed to an anatomic or normal variant, which occurs in greater than 1% of the general population. [3] For this discussion, the term anomaly will be used interchangeably for both entities.

retrieved_docs.title: Bicornuate Uterus -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 2021, the American Society of Reproductive Medicine (ASRM) Müllerian Anomalies Classification created 9 classifications: Cervical agenesis Müllerian agenesis Unicornuate uterus Bicornuate uterus Septate uterus Uterus didelphys Longitucinal vaginal septum Transverse vaginal septum Complex anomalies [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Clubfoot -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetics likely has a role to play, although a specific gene alteration remains unclear. Research shows that 24.4% of cases have an associated family history, though it has not identified a definite mode of inheritance. [8] Studies of monozygotic twins have shown a concordance of 33% in comparison to 3% in dizygotic twins. [9] In 20% of cases, clubfoot presents in association with at least one of the following conditions [9] : Distal arthrogryposis Congenital myotonic dystrophy Myelomeningocele Amniotic band sequence Any other associated genetic syndrome Trisomy 18 Chromosome 22q11 deletion syndrome Approximately 7% of clubfoot cases will have another anomaly at birth, and 7.6% will have a neurodevelopmental condition of some degree. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2270
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical presentation described as “Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” is used to define and classify that same set of perinatal abnormalities, and it is categorized under the term “تقييد النمو الجنيني,” which translates to fetal growth restriction. This grouping emphasizes that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are collectively recognized as a single entity of fetal growth restriction in medical classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: CDISC SEND Terminology includes digestive congenital anomalies as a subset concept within its classification system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 547
retrieved_docs.content: The entity “CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION” is defined as a parent category that includes the combined condition “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation.” This combined condition is used as both a descriptor and a classification label for itself, emphasizing its role as a distinct clinical grouping. The specific diagnosis “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” (fetal undernutrition without reference to being light or small for gestational age) is classified under the “short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation” grouping, indicating that it shares the same underlying features of premature delivery, reduced birth weight, and impaired fetal growth. In addition, “Foetale ondervoeding zonder vermelding van licht of klein voor zwangerschapsduur” has a broader relationship to the Arabic‑language concept “اضطراب لدى الأطفال وحديثي الولادة,” which denotes a disorder affecting infants and newborns. Together, these relationships illustrate how fetal growth restriction, prematurity, and low birth weight are interconnected components of fetal malnutrition and form part of a larger spectrum of neonatal disorders, underscoring the importance of recognizing these linked conditions for comprehensive perinatal assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) constitute a broad diagnostic category that includes, as a child entity, Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). Within the same overarching group, the condition Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation is linked as a parent concept, indicating that it is a more specific manifestation of perinatal disorders. This condition is classified both as تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction) and as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (extremely low birth weight, unspecified), reflecting its dual characterization in terms of growth limitation and birth‑weight severity. The concept تقييد النمو الجنيني has a broader relationship to حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), suggesting that fetal growth restriction is part of a larger, less defined set of perinatal abnormalities. In

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of a liveborn infant encompasses a range of perinatal classifications that are linked through gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth status. A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a specific child category of liveborn, indicating that infants born alive at this stage are included within the broader liveborn group. The duration of gestation has an inverse‑isa relationship with liveborn, reflecting that shorter gestational periods are associated with distinct clinical classifications of liveborn infants. One major classification of liveborn infants is short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This category both classifies liveborn and is self‑referential, emphasizing its central role in describing infants born with reduced gestational length, low birth weight, or impaired growth. The disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight is positioned as a parent of liveborn, indicating that this disorder underlies the

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: The network links gestational timing, birth outcomes, and perinatal pathology through multiple classification systems. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is recorded as a **liveborn** outcome; liveborn is a subtype of **Duration of gestation**, which is coded within the **International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM)**. Within ICD‑10‑AM, **Duration of gestation** has the child concept **اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ** (“respiratory disorder”). The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** (Arabic: تقييد النمو الجنيني and ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}) both classifies and is classified by **liveborn**, designating infants with these characteristics as a specific subgroup of liveborn births. This condition serves as the parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes two child entities:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: Conditions of fetal growth and malnutrition are regarded as a parent category that includes the specific outcomes of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. The grouping of short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation serves as a classification label for these adverse perinatal conditions, emphasizing their interrelated nature in the context of impaired fetal development and inadequate nutrition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION encompass the specific subgroup identified as short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation. This subgroup is both defined by and classified under the term تقييد النمو الجنيني (fetal growth restriction). تقييد النمو الجنيني, in turn, is linked to a broader, unspecified condition that arises in the perinatal period, described as حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة. Together, these relationships illustrate that short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized components of fetal growth restriction, which itself forms part of a larger, not‑yet‑specified perinatal disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2019
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Data Interchange Standards Consortium Terminology defines a specific subset that includes concepts related to digestive congenital anomalies, indicating that within the CDISC framework these anomalies are organized as a distinct category to support standardized representation of associated clinical concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: (unspecified condition originating in the perinatal period), situating fetal growth restriction within a larger, undefined perinatal condition category. Together, these relationships illustrate how gestational duration, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, and associated perinatal disorders are organized within medical classification systems, aiding clinicians in diagnosing and managing newborns with short gestation and related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 151
retrieved_docs.content: The concepts describe a hierarchical classification of perinatal and neonatal conditions centered on gestational age and birth outcomes. A gestational period of **29‑30 weeks of gestation completed** is linked to the outcome of a **liveborn** infant, indicating that births occurring at this gestational age are considered liveborn. The broader category **Duration of gestation** is inversely related to **liveborn**, reflecting that liveborn status is defined by the length of gestation. The condition **Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation** serves as a classifier for **liveborn** births and is itself classified as **تقييد النمو الجنيني** (fetal growth restriction). This condition also functions as a parent category for **Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96)**, which in turn includes **Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29)** as a child. **CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION** is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 388
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period described as “29‑30 weeks of gestation completed” is classified under the Arabic term خديج (preterm birth) and is linked to a liveborn outcome. Preterm births (خديج) are frequently caused by متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). The concept of Duration of gestation is a key attribute of liveborn infants and is organized within the International Statistical Classification of Diseases and Related Health Problems, 10th Revision, Australian Modification (ICD‑10‑AM). Within this classification system, respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) are listed as a child category, reflecting the close relationship between gestational age, preterm status, underlying pulmonary immaturity, and respiratory complications. Recognizing these interconnections and their coding in ICD‑10‑AM supports accurate diagnosis, documentation, and clinical management of infants born at 29–30 weeks gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.
